## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Immediate referral - Suspected internal bleeding (Concealed PPH). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check the belly**: If it is growing larger or feels very hard, it may be bleeding inside.
- **Position her on her left side** to help with blood flow.
- Ensure the baby is suckling.
- Do NOT give any food; give only sips of water.
- **Rush to the hospital**; internal bleeding can happen even if nothing is coming out of the vagina.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Sharp upper belly pain; no placenta"] --> B["Check if the belly is growing larger/harder"]
  B --> C["Position mother on her left side"]
  C --> D["Encourage breastfeeding to help contractions"]
  D --> E["Do NOT allow any heavy movement or food"]
  E --> F["Immediate transport to District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sharp high pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Belly growing | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Placenta stuck | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild back ache | Refer for checkup | Rest and monitor |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bleeding can occur behind the placenta (Concealed hemorrhage). The mother can die of blood loss even if there is no visible bleeding.

query: The woman says she has to go to the toilet to pass the placenta. Should I let her walk to the latrine?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Attempting to walk with a retained placenta**
- Dizziness or fainting when standing up
- Heavy bleeding gush upon standing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Prohibit walking. Use a bedpan or squatting position on the floor. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT let her walk.** She may faint and fall, causing head injury or massive bleeding.
- Help her **squat right where she is** (on a clean sheet or bedpan).
- Squatting is better than lying down for the placenta to come out.
- **Give her support** (hold her under the arms) so she doesn't fall.
- If it doesn't come out after 5 minutes of squatting, have her lie back down and **refer to CHC**.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Mother wants to walk to toilet for placenta"] --> B["Do NOT allow her to walk to the latrine"]
  B --> C["Assist her to squat on a clean sheet on the floor"]
  C --> D["Provide physical support so she doesn't fall"]
  D --> E["If placenta fails to pass, return her to bed"]
  E --> F["Refer to the nearest hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dizziness on standing | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Placenta stuck | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Heavy bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Urge to urinate | Use bedpan/squat | Do not let her walk |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Standing up causes a sudden drop in blood pressure and a gush of blood. Postpartum mothers should never walk until the placenta is out and bleeding is stable.

query: Can we give the mother a lot of water to drink to push the 'aonl' (placenta) out? Her belly is very soft.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Soft, boggy belly (Uterine Atony)**
- Placenta not out after 30 minutes
- Slow but steady trickling of blood

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Massage the belly (Fundal Massage) and immediate referral. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Drinking water will not push the placenta out.** Instead, it may make her vomit.
- **Belly Massage**: Use your hand to rub the belly in a circular motion until it feels like a hard cricket ball.
- Keep the baby on the breast.
- If the belly stays soft after 10 minutes of massage, she is at high risk of PPH.
- **Start transport to the hospital** while continuing to massage the belly.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Soft belly and stuck placenta"] --> B["Begin firm circular massage on the belly"]
  B --> C["Continue massage until womb feels like a hard ball"]
  C --> D["Put baby to breast to trigger natural hormones"]
  D --> E["Do NOT force-feed large amounts of water"]
  E --> F["Refer to hospital while continuing belly massage"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Soft/Boggy belly | Immediate Referral | Belly massage |
| Stuck placenta | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Hard womb | Monitor at home | If placenta is out |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A soft belly means the womb is not contracting. This is the most common cause of death after childbirth. Massage is first aid, but hospital medicine is needed.

query: The mother is 18 years old. This is her first baby. The placenta is stuck and she is crying in severe pain. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe pain after the baby is out**
- Hysterical crying or panic
- Heavy bleeding
- Placenta not out within 30 minutes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Young Mother (Primi) | Female | Pain management via comfort and urgent referral for suspected complications. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Calm her down**: Panic makes the heart beat faster and increases bleeding.
- Tell her to take **slow, deep breaths**.
- Check if she has any **tears or cuts** in the birth canal that are causing pain.
- Ensure she is warm and her bladder is empty.
- **Rush to the hospital**; in young mothers, the placenta may be attached too deeply (Placenta Accreta) or there may be internal damage.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Young mother with severe pain and stuck placenta"] --> B["Calm the mother and encourage deep breathing"]
  B --> C["Check for vaginal tears or heavy bleeding"]
  C --> D["Ensure the bladder is empty and baby is nursing"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any painkillers by mouth"]
  E --> F["Immediate referral to District Hospital for evaluation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Placenta stuck | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Heavy bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Panic/Agitation | Immediate Referral | Calm environment |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Severe pain while the placenta is retained can indicate an internal tear or a placenta that is stuck to the womb wall. Both need surgery.

query: The baby was born 2 hours ago. Placenta is still inside. No bleeding at all. Can we wait until tomorrow morning?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Placenta retained over 2 hours**
- Sudden, delayed gush of blood (Late PPH)
- Fever or chills starting
- Bad smell from the vagina

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Do NOT wait. Immediate referral for manual removal and antibiotics. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Never wait until morning.** The risk of sudden heavy bleeding or severe infection increases every minute.
- Keep her lying down and monitor her pulse.
- Ensure the baby is nursing frequently.
- **Refer to the hospital NOW.** Even if there is no bleeding, the placenta is a piece of 'dead tissue' that will rot and cause infection if not removed.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Placenta inside for 2 hours; no bleeding"] --> B["Explain the risk of sudden bleeding to the family"]
  B --> C["Prohibit waiting until morning; move now"]
  C --> D["Check for early signs of fever or foul smell"]
  D --> E["Ensure the baby stays with the mother during travel"]
  E --> F["Immediate transport to the nearest CHC or hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Time > 2 hours | Emergency Referral | Never |
| No bleeding | Still refer immediately | Do not wait |
| Foul smell | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever/Chills | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A 'silent' retained placenta is a ticking time bomb for infection and sudden massive hemorrhage.

query: Is there a specific way to massage the belly to get the placenta out?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Soft, squishy belly**
- Blood clots passing during massage
- Placenta not delivered

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Perform firm Fundal Massage and refer immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Fundal Massage**: Place one hand at the bottom of the belly (just above the pubic bone) and the other at the top.
- **Rub firmly in a circle** on the top of the womb until it feels hard and round.
- Stop once it is hard; do not over-massage if it's already firm.
- Massage every 15 minutes to make sure it stays hard.
- **Refer to hospital** while continuing to monitor the womb's hardness.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Placenta stuck; womb feels soft"] --> B["Support bottom of womb with one hand"]
  B --> C["Rub the top of the womb firmly in circles"]
  C --> D["Continue until the womb feels like a hard ball"]
  D --> E["Check for hardness every 15 minutes"]
  E --> F["Transport to hospital for placenta removal"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Womb stays soft | Emergency Referral | Continue massage |
| Placenta stuck | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Heavy bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Womb is hard | Refer for placenta | Continue monitoring |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Massage is a first-aid step to stop bleeding, but it will not always release the placenta if it is stuck to the wall. Medical help is required.

query: A woman has a retained placenta and is also very anemic (pale). What is the highest risk?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe paleness + Retained placenta**
- Pulse over 100 beats per minute
- Fast breathing
- Very low energy

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Anemic Mother | Female | Highest priority referral. She has zero 'reserve' if she bleeds. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Treat as a Level 1 Emergency.** Anemic women can die from even a small amount of blood loss.
- **Do NOT allow her to exert herself**; keep her completely flat.
- Give **sweetened lemon water** or juice for sugar support.
- Arrange for **2-3 family blood donors** to travel with her to the hospital.
- **Rush to a District Hospital** with a blood bank immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Anemic mother with stuck placenta"] --> B["Lay her flat and prohibit all physical movement"]
  B --> C["Provide sweetened drinks if fully conscious"]
  C --> D["Identify family members with the same blood group"]
  D --> E["Call for ambulance for immediate transport"]
  E --> F["Transfer directly to hospital with blood bank"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Anemia + Placenta | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Weak pulse | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fainting | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild paleness | Emergency Referral | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In anemic mothers, the 'Golden Hour' is much shorter. Every drop of blood lost is critical.

query: The placenta is partially out but hanging. The mother is in a lot of pain and there is a lot of blood. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Placenta hanging partially out**
- Active, bright red bleeding
- Severe cramping
- Fainting spells

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Support the tissue and refer immediately to a surgical center. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT pull** the hanging part. It may be attached to a piece of the womb.
- Use a **clean, dry cloth** to gently support the hanging tissue so it doesn't drag.
- Have the mother **squat and cough** to see if it slips out naturally.
- Keep her lying down if she is bleeding heavily.
- **Rush to the hospital**; they need to remove the rest manually under sterile conditions.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Placenta partially hanging out"] --> B["Do NOT pull or tug the hanging portion"]
  B --> C["Support tissue with a clean, dry cloth"]
  C --> D["Try squatting and coughing once"]
  D --> E["Monitor for heavy bleeding and fainting"]
  E --> F["Immediate transport to the nearest CHC/Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hanging placenta | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Heavy bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Severe pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Tissue passes | Still refer for check | Save tissue for doctor |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A partially delivered placenta often prevents the womb from closing, leading to massive blood loss. It must be fully removed at a hospital.

query: ASHA worker asking: The mother's belly is very hard and she says it feels like she is having another baby inside, but it's just the placenta stuck. Is this normal?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme, labor-like pain after delivery**
- Womb staying hard but placenta not moving
- Signs of shock (cold skin/fast pulse)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Immediate referral. The womb is trying but the placenta is 'trapped'. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check if there is another baby** (Twins); feel the belly for a second head or bottom.
- If it's just the placenta, the cervix may have closed early, trapping it (Hourglass contraction).
- Do NOT give her any more medicine to make the womb contract (like misoprostol) until seen by a doctor.
- Give **warm Ajwain (Carom) water** to help her relax.
- **Refer to the hospital** for manual removal.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Severe pain; placenta trapped in hard womb"] --> B["Check belly to ensure no second baby is inside"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any contraction-inducing medicine"]
  C --> D["Provide warm ajwain water for comfort"]
  D --> E["Monitor for heavy bleeding or shock"]
  E --> F["Immediate referral to CHC for manual removal"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Stuck placenta | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Hard womb, no delivery | Emergency Referral | Not safe at home |
| Mild cramps | Refer for placenta | Monitor closely |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A 'trapped' placenta requires specialized medical maneuvers to relax the cervix and remove the tissue. Do not try this at home.

query: The mother has a retained placenta and she is starting to talk nonsense and acting confused. What is the priority?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Confusion or talking nonsense (Sign of brain shock)**
- Bluish lips or nails
- No pulse felt at the wrist
- Heavy bleeding

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Critical Emergency - Life-saving referral for hemorrhagic shock. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Priority: Airway and Circulation.**
- **Lay her flat and elevate her legs** to the highest possible level.
- Turn her head to the side if she vomits.
- **Do NOT give anything by mouth** (risk of choking).
- **Call 108 and rush to the hospital.** Tell the ambulance she is in 'Shock due to blood loss'.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Mother is confused/agitated; placenta stuck"] --> B["Lay her flat and elevate legs immediately"]
  B --> C["Turn head to side and clear any vomit"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give food or water by mouth"]
  D --> E["Check for visible heavy bleeding"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confusion/Delirium | Emergency Referral | Never |
| No wrist pulse | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Blue lips | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Agitation | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Confusion in a postpartum mother with a retained placenta is a sign of massive internal or external blood loss. Every second counts.

query: The placenta has been inside for 45 minutes and the baby is not breathing well. Who should I help first?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Baby not breathing/weak cry**
- **Mother with retained placenta**
- Heavy bleeding from the mother

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Refer both. Mother: nursing baby. Baby: stimulation/warmth. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Help the baby first**: Rub the baby's back and soles of the feet to make them cry. Keep the baby warm.
- **Skin-to-skin**: Once the baby breathes, place the baby on the mother's chest (skin-to-skin). This helps the baby AND helps the placenta come out.
- **Call for help**: You need two people—one for the mother and one for the baby.
- **Refer both immediately** to the CHC in the same vehicle.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Baby not breathing + stuck placenta"] --> B["Stimulate baby by rubbing back and feet"]
  B --> C["Place breathing baby on mother's chest (skin-to-skin)"]
  C --> D["Encourage breastfeeding to help placenta"]
  D --> E["Keep both mother and baby warm with blankets"]
  E --> F["Emergency transport for both to the nearest CHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Baby not crying | Emergency Referral | Stimulate at home |
| Stuck placenta | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Both unwell | Emergency Referral | Call for 2 assistants |
| Mother bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Both situations are emergencies. Skin-to-skin contact is the best 'natural medicine' to stabilize both while moving to the hospital.

query: Can I give the mother a lot of Salt and Sugar water (ORS) if the placenta is stuck and she is bleeding?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding with stuck placenta**
- Feeling very thirsty (sign of blood loss)
- Dizziness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Provide ORS for hydration while waiting for IV support at hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Yes, give ORS or Salt-Sugar-Lemon water** if she is fully awake and not vomiting.
- This helps maintain her blood pressure while moving to the hospital.
- Give small, frequent sips rather than a large amount at once.
- Continue **belly massage** and **breastfeeding**.
- **Transport to hospital** immediately; water cannot replace the need for a blood transfusion or surgery.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bleeding mother with stuck placenta"] --> B["Check if she is awake and can swallow safely"]
  B --> C["Give small frequent sips of ORS or salt-sugar water"]
  C --> D["Continue firm belly massage to keep womb hard"]
  D --> E["Encourage baby to continue suckling"]
  E --> F["Immediate referral to CHC for IV fluids/Blood"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Excessive thirst | Emergency Referral | Give ORS sips |
| Stuck placenta | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Heavy bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fainting | Emergency Referral | Do not give water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Oral fluids are only a temporary support. If a mother has lost significant blood, only IV fluids and blood at a hospital can save her.

query: The mother is 40 years old and has 6 children. This baby is her 7th. Placenta is stuck. Is she at higher risk?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Mother with many previous births (Grand Multipara)**
- Placenta not out within 30 minutes
- Very fast heart rate
- Belly feels like a 'bag of water' (very soft)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Older Mother (Multipara) | Female | Extremely high risk. Immediate referral before bleeding starts. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT wait for bleeding.** Mothers with many children have 'tired' wombs that often don't contract at all.
- Start **firm belly massage** immediately after the baby is out.
- Ensure the baby is nursing.
- **Refer to a District Hospital** right away; she is at the highest risk for massive, sudden PPH.
- Keep her lying flat and warm during the journey.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Older mother (7th baby) with stuck placenta"] --> B["Start firm belly massage immediately"] 
  B --> C["Explain high risk of bleeding to the family"]
  C --> D["Prohibit any physical exertion for the mother"]
  D --> E["Encourage immediate breastfeeding for hormones"]
  E --> F["Direct transport to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Grand Multipara (Many babies) | Immediate Referral | Never deliver at home |
| Stuck placenta | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Soft belly | Emergency Referral | Constant massage |
| Normal energy | Still refer immediately | High risk status |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Mothers with many previous births are the most likely to die from retained placenta/PPH. They must always be delivered in a hospital.

query: The baby was born in the field/outside and the placenta is stuck. It is dirty and cold. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Placenta stuck after delivery in unhygienic place**
- Cold skin (Hypothermia)
- Shivering
- Visible dirt near the birth canal

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Warmth, external cleaning, and immediate referral. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep her warm**: Use any clean dry clothes to cover the mother and baby.
- **Clean the outside**: Gently wipe away visible mud or dirt from the thighs and outside of the vagina with a clean cloth. Do NOT clean inside.
- Do NOT try to pull the placenta out in the field.
- **Move her to the hospital** immediately; she needs a Tetanus injection and antibiotics immediately along with placenta removal.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Birth in field; stuck placenta"] --> B["Cover mother and baby with clean dry clothes"]
  B --> C["Wipe external dirt away with clean cloth"]
  C --> D["Do NOT pull the cord or insert fingers"]
  D --> E["Move to a vehicle and keep mother lying down"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC for Tetanus/Antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Field birth | Emergency Referral | External cleaning only |
| Stuck placenta | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Shivering | Emergency Referral | Keep warm |
| Dirt in birth canal | Emergency Referral | Never clean inside |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Birth in an open field carries a very high risk of Tetanus and Sepsis. Both mother and baby need urgent medical protection.

query: A mother who gave birth 4 days ago has a very high fever and shivering. Her discharge smells very bad and her lower stomach hurts when touched. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever higher than 102°F with chills/shivering**
- **Foul-smelling or pus-like vaginal discharge**
- Severe pain or tenderness in the lower abdomen
- Fast heart rate and rapid breathing
- Fainting or extreme dizziness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Provide fluids, sponge for fever, and arrange immediate hospital transport for antibiotics. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Perform Tepid Sponging**: Wipe the mother's forehead, neck, and armpits with a cloth soaked in room-temperature water to bring down the fever.
- **Encourage Hydration**: Give her ORS, Coconut water, or plain boiled water to prevent dehydration from fever.
- **Ensure Hygiene**: Change her sanitary pads frequently and use only clean, boiled, and dried cotton cloths.
- **Do NOT** apply any local herbs, oils, or pastes inside the vagina.
- **Call 108** or arrange transport to the nearest CHC immediately; she needs IV antibiotics.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Mother has high fever and foul-smelling discharge"] --> B["Check temperature and perform tepid sponging"]
  B --> C["Give fluids like ORS or coconut water for hydration"]
  C --> D["Ensure use of clean pads and maintain perineal hygiene"]
  D --> E["Do NOT insert any home remedies into the vagina"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately for antibiotic treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Foul smell/Pus | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Shivering/High Fever | Emergency Referral | Sponge to cool during travel |
| Severe belly pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild body ache | Refer to PHC | Rest and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infection after birth (Puerperal Sepsis) can spread to the blood very quickly. Home remedies are only for comfort; medical treatment is vital to save the mother's life.

query: The stitches from the delivery have become red, swollen, and are leaking yellow fluid. The mother can't even sit down. Help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Pus (yellow/green fluid) leaking from stitches**
- **Stitches opening up (Gaping)**
- Redness spreading to the thighs or buttocks
- Severe throbbing pain at the stitch site
- High fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Keep the area clean and dry, then refer to the doctor who did the stitches. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sitz Bath (Local Care)**: Have the mother sit in a basin of clean, warm water with a pinch of salt for 10 minutes to soothe the area.
- **Keep it Dry**: After the wash, gently pat the area dry with a clean, soft cloth. Do not rub.
- **Avoid Sitting Directly**: Use a folded towel or 'donut' cushion to keep pressure off the stitches.
- Do NOT apply turmeric paste or oils to the infected stitches.
- **Refer to the PHC/CHC** immediately for wound cleaning and oral/IV antibiotics.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Swollen, red stitches with pus leakage"] --> B["Provide warm salt-water sitz bath for 10 minutes"]
  B --> C["Gently pat the area dry with a clean cloth"]
  C --> D["Ensure mother does not apply any local pastes or oils"]
  D --> E["Check for fever or spreading redness"]
  E --> F["Refer to the hospital for wound care and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pus leakage | Immediate Referral | Salt water sitz bath |
| Stitches opening | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Spreading redness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild itching | Refer to PHC | Keep area clean and dry |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infected stitches (Episiotomy infection) can lead to deep tissue infection. Professional wound dressing and antibiotics are required.

query: A mother is 1 week postpartum. Her breasts are very hard, red, and hot to touch. She has a high fever and is crying in pain. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Breasts are extremely hard (Engorged) and bright red**
- **A defined, hard, painful lump** (Abscess)
- High fever with shivering
- Red streaks spreading away from the breast

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Lactating Mother | Female | Continue breastfeeding/expressing milk and refer to CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Continue Breastfeeding**: If it is too painful, gently express the milk by hand. Stopping milk flow makes the infection worse.
- **Warm Compress**: Apply a warm, clean cloth to the breast for 10 minutes before feeding to help milk flow.
- **Cold Compress**: Apply a cold cloth *after* feeding to reduce pain and swelling.
- Use **Cabbage leaves** (local remedy) placed inside the bra; it can help reduce swelling.
- **Refer to the doctor**; she may need antibiotics or a small procedure to drain pus.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Hard, red, painful breasts with fever"] --> B["Encourage frequent breastfeeding or hand expression"]
  B --> C["Apply warm compress before feeding to help flow"]
  C --> D["Apply cold compress after feeding to reduce pain"]
  D --> E["Check for hard lumps or pus from the nipple"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for antibiotics or abscess drainage"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| High fever/Chills | Immediate Referral | Continue milk expression |
| Hard, painful lump | Immediate Referral | Warm compress |
| Red streaks | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fullness, no fever | Home Care | Frequent breastfeeding |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Breast infection (Mastitis) can quickly turn into an abscess (collection of pus) if the milk is not removed and antibiotics are not started.

query: The mother is 10 days after delivery. She is very confused, breathing fast, and her hands are cold, but she has a very high fever. No major bleeding. What is the priority?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Confusion or talking nonsense**
- **Cold hands and feet with high fever**
- Rapid, shallow breathing
- No urine passed in 8 hours

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Critical Emergency - Treat for Septic Shock and rush to District Hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Lay her flat** and elevate her legs 12 inches to keep blood flow to the brain.
- **Keep her warm** with blankets; cold hands in a fever patient is a sign of shock.
- Do NOT give anything to drink if she is confused (risk of choking).
- **Call 108 immediately**; she needs urgent IV fluids and life-saving antibiotics.
- Note the time she became confused for the doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Confusion and cold skin with high fever"] --> B["Lay mother flat and elevate her legs 12 inches"]
  B --> C["Cover with blankets to maintain body heat"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give any food or water by mouth"]
  D --> E["Check for any foul-smelling discharge"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital for Sepsis"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confusion/Delirium | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cold skin + Fever | Emergency Referral | Keep warm |
| Rapid breathing | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever only | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is Septic Shock. The infection is in the blood. Only a hospital with intensive care can save her life at this stage.

query: ASHA worker reporting: A mother who delivered at home 5 days ago has a fever. The family is giving her only 'dry' food and no water to 'dry the wound'. She is very weak. Help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever with signs of dehydration** (sunken eyes, dry mouth)
- Severe weakness/cannot stand
- Dark-colored urine or no urine
- High fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Correct the diet immediately and refer for infection check. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Educate the family**: A mother *needs* more water and nutritious food to heal and fight infection. 'Drying the wound' by stopping water is dangerous.
- Give her **ORS, Dal water, or Coconut water** immediately.
- Provide **warm, fresh Khichdi** or soft rice with ghee.
- Check for foul smell from her discharge or belly pain.
- **Refer to PHC**; she is too weak to fight the infection without help.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Weak mother with fever and restricted diet"] --> B["Explain to family that water and food are needed for healing"]
  B --> C["Give ORS and warm nutritious fluids immediately"]
  C --> D["Check for belly pain or foul-smelling discharge"]
  D --> E["Perform tepid sponging to lower the fever"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for infection treatment and hydration"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe dehydration | Immediate Referral | Give fluids during travel |
| Fever > 101°F | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Weakness | Refer to PHC | Nutritious food and fluids |
| Normal energy | Routine checkup | Proper diet/water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Restricting water and food to a postpartum mother with an infection will lead to kidney failure and death. Hygiene and nutrition are basic medicine.

query: The mother has a high fever and is complaining of pain when she passes urine. She delivered 3 days ago. What are the steps?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Burning pain during urination**
- Pain in the lower back (kidney area)
- Fever with shivering
- Blood in the urine
- Frequent urge to go but only a few drops come out

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Increase fluid intake and refer for UTI checkup. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Flush the system**: Tell her to drink 1 glass of water every hour.
- Give **Barley (Jau) water** or **Cranberry/Amla juice** if available; these help clean the urinary path.
- Maintain strict **perineal hygiene**: Wash from front to back after using the toilet.
- Use **warm water for washing** the private area.
- **Refer to PHC** for a urine test and antibiotics.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Painful urination and fever after delivery"] --> B["Increase water intake to one glass every hour"]
  B --> C["Provide barley water or amla juice"]
  C --> D["Teach cleaning from front to back after toilet use"]
  D --> E["Check for lower back pain or fever"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for urine test and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever + Back pain | Immediate Referral | Increase water intake |
| Blood in urine | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Painful urination | Refer today | Drink plenty of fluids |
| Mild discomfort | Refer if not better in 12h | Hygiene and water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Urinary tract infections (UTI) after delivery can quickly reach the kidneys if not treated with proper antibiotics.

query: A mother had a C-section in the city and came back to the village. Now the belly wound is red and a clear-pink fluid is leaking. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Gaping of the C-section wound**
- **Pink or yellow fluid leaking from the cut**
- Redness or heat spreading around the wound
- Foul smell from the dressing
- Severe pain when moving

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Post-Surgical Mother | Female | Cover with sterile gauze, do not touch, and refer to the hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT touch the wound** with bare hands. This adds more germs.
- **Cover the area** with a clean, dry, sterile gauze or a freshly ironed clean cloth.
- Tell the mother to **avoid lifting any weight** (except the baby).
- Keep the dressing **dry**; do not apply water or local oils to the cut.
- **Refer to the hospital** where the surgery was done immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["C-section wound leaking fluid or red"] --> B["Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Cover wound with sterile gauze or clean dry cloth"]
  C --> D["Prohibit lifting weights or applying local oils"]
  D --> E["Check for fever or worsening pain"]
  E --> F["Refer immediately to the hospital of surgery"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fluid leaking | Immediate Referral | Keep dry and covered |
| Wound opening | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Redness/Pain | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Itching (healing) | Refer if red | Keep clean and dry |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
C-section wound infections can lead to deep internal infections (Peritonitis). A doctor must examine the depth of the infection.

query: 12 days after birth. Mother has a mild fever. She says her legs hurt, and one calf is red and swollen. Is this infection?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **One leg swollen and red, the other is normal**
- **Pain in the calf** when walking or flexing the foot
- Heat in the swollen leg area
- Shortness of breath (Emergency sign)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Absolute rest, do NOT massage, and refer immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT massage the leg.** This is the most important rule. Massaging can move a blood clot to the heart or lungs.
- **Absolute Bed Rest**: Tell her not to walk or move the leg.
- Keep the leg **slightly raised** on a pillow.
- Do not apply any hot or cold packs.
- **Rush to a hospital**; she needs an ultrasound and blood thinners, not just antibiotics.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["One-sided leg pain and swelling after delivery"] --> B["Stop any attempts to massage the leg immediately"]
  B --> C["Enforce absolute bed rest; do not let her walk"]
  C --> D["Elevate the leg slightly on a soft pillow"]
  D --> E["Check for chest pain or breathing difficulty"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to hospital for clot check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| One-sided swelling | Emergency Referral | Never massage |
| Calf pain | Emergency Referral | Absolute rest |
| Shortness of breath | Critical Emergency | Call 108 |
| General leg ache | Refer to PHC | Rest and monitor |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This may be a blood clot (DVT) which can feel like an infection. If the clot moves to the lungs, it can kill the mother in minutes.

query: A mother has a fever and her stomach is very tender. She says she passed a piece of 'meat' (tissue) and since then the smell is very bad. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Passing pieces of tissue (Placenta fragments)**
- **Extremely foul smell** from the vagina
- Severe pain in the lower belly
- Continuous heavy bleeding

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Save the tissue and refer to the hospital for womb cleaning (D&C). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Save the tissue**: Put the passed piece in a clean bag or container for the doctor to see.
- **Massage the belly**: Gently rub the top of the womb to help it stay hard and push out any remaining pieces.
- Give **warm Jaggery (Gur) water** for energy.
- Use **clean cotton pads** and count how many she soaks.
- **Refer to CHC** immediately; she needs the remaining pieces removed to stop the infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Tissue passed with foul smell and fever"] --> B["Save the passed tissue in a clean container"]
  B --> C["Gently massage the top of the womb (fundus)"]
  C --> D["Ensure high hydration with warm fluids"]
  D --> E["Check for heavy vaginal bleeding"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for manual cleaning and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Retained tissue | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Foul-smelling bleed | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Severe belly pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Retained placenta pieces rot inside the womb, causing severe infection and bleeding. A hospital procedure is the only cure.

query: A village woman had a delivery 2 weeks ago. Now she has a fever and a very painful, red lump in her groin area (near the thigh). What is the first aid?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Painful, swollen lump in the groin (Lymph node)**
- High fever
- Red streaks on the skin of the legs or belly
- Pus or sores in the private area

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Do not squeeze the lump. Refer for systemic infection check. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT squeeze or press the lump**; this can spread the infection into the blood.
- **Apply a warm, clean cloth** (warm compress) to the area to help with the pain.
- Keep the mother **resting**; physical movement can spread infection through the lymph system.
- Give her **warm fluids** and light food.
- **Refer to PHC** to find the source of the infection (vagina, bladder, or leg).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Painful groin lump and fever after delivery"] --> B["Do NOT squeeze or poke the swollen lump"]
  B --> C["Apply a clean warm compress to the area for pain"]
  C --> D["Perform tepid sponging to lower fever"]
  D --> E["Check for vaginal discharge or urinary pain"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for infection source checkup"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swollen groin lump | Refer to PHC | Warm compress |
| High fever | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Red streaks | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Localized small sore | Refer to PHC | Hygiene and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A swollen groin lump means the body is fighting a significant infection nearby. It requires professional medical assessment.

query: The mother is shivering uncontrollably 2 days after birth. She is too weak to hold the baby. No fever yet, but she feels cold. Steps?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Uncontrollable shivering (Rigors)**
- Extreme weakness (cannot lift the baby)
- Cold skin despite shivering
- Rapid heart rate

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| New Mother | Female | Keep warm and monitor temperature closely. Refer if rigors continue. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cover with blankets** and use hot water bottles (wrapped in cloth) near the feet.
- Give her a **hot drink** like Ginger tea or warm milk.
- **Skin-to-Skin**: Let someone else hold the baby skin-to-skin to keep the baby warm while the mother is unwell.
- **Check temperature every 30 minutes**; a high fever usually follows shivering.
- **Refer to CHC**; rigors often mean bacteria have entered the bloodstream.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Uncontrollable shivering after delivery"] --> B["Cover mother with blankets and keep her warm"]
  B --> C["Provide a hot drink like ginger tea"]
  C --> D["Monitor temperature every 30 minutes"]
  D --> E["Ensure the baby is safe and warm with another adult"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC as high fever and infection are likely"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Continued shivering | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Extreme weakness | Immediate Referral | Not safe at home |
| Fever starts | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Mild chills only | Refer if fever starts | Warmth and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Shivering (Rigors) is a classic sign of blood infection. Do not wait for a fever to appear before seeking help.

query: A mother delivered in the hospital but has come home. Today she has a headache, fever, and her neck is very stiff. She can't look at the light. What is the emergency?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stiff neck (cannot touch chin to chest)**
- **Light sensitivity (pain in eyes with light)**
- Severe headache and fever
- Confusion or vomiting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Critical Emergency - Keep in a dark room and rush to District Hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Place in a dark, quiet room** immediately to reduce eye pain and seizure risk.
- Do NOT give any food or water by mouth.
- **Lay her on her side** (recovery position) in case she vomits.
- Apply a **cool damp cloth** to her forehead.
- **Call 108 immediately**; she needs a spinal check and IV medicines for brain/spinal infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stiff neck, headache, and fever after delivery"] --> B["Place mother in a dark, quiet room immediately"]
  B --> C["Lay her on her side to prevent choking if vomiting"]
  C --> D["Perform cool sponging for the fever"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give anything to eat or drink"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stiff neck | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Light sensitivity | Emergency Referral | Dark room |
| Confusion | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Headache + Fever | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is a sign of Meningitis or severe Sepsis. It is a life-threatening emergency that needs advanced hospital care.

query: Can I give the mother Neem water wash for her private parts after delivery to prevent infection?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Redness, swelling, or pus in the birth canal area**
- Foul smell
- Fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Use only boiled, cooled water for cleaning. Do not put anything *inside*. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **External Wash ONLY**: You can use boiled and cooled Neem water to wash the *outside* (perineum). Neem is a natural antiseptic.
- **Do NOT wash inside** the vagina. The womb is an open wound and needs to heal naturally.
- **Dry thoroughly**: After washing, pat the area dry with a clean, soft cloth.
- Use **Sun-dried, clean cotton cloths** for pads.
- If there is already a bad smell or fever, **refer to PHC** immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Postpartum hygiene query"] --> B["Advise external washing with boiled cooled water only"]
  B --> C["Neem water is okay for external use only"]
  C --> D["Strictly prohibit washing inside the vagina"]
  D --> E["Teach importance of using clean, sun-dried pads"]
  E --> F["Refer if any redness, pus, or bad smell appears"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bad smell/Fever | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Redness/Swelling | Refer to PHC | External hygiene |
| Normal healing | Home Care | Cleanliness and rest |
| Pus from stitches | Immediate Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Internal washing (douching) actually *causes* infection by pushing germs into the womb. Only external hygiene is safe.

query: A mother delivered 6 days ago. She has a cough, fast breathing, and a high fever. Her family says it's a 'cold', but I am worried. What should I check?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fast breathing (more than 30 breaths per minute)**
- **Coughing up yellow or green phlegm**
- High fever with chest pain
- Blue lips or nails

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Immediate referral to rule out Pneumonia or Sepsis. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sit her up**: Prop her up with pillows; do not let her lie flat as it makes breathing harder.
- **Provide fresh air**: Open windows and clear the room of people.
- Give **warm Ginger and Tulsi water** to soothe the cough.
- **Monitor the fever**: Use tepid sponging if it's very high.
- **Refer to CHC** immediately; infection in the lungs after birth is very dangerous for a weak mother.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Fast breathing, cough, and fever after delivery"] --> B["Sit the mother upright with back support"]
  B --> C["Ensure plenty of fresh air and ventilation"]
  C --> D["Provide warm ginger-tulsi water for cough relief"]
  D --> E["Perform tepid sponging for high fever"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for lung and blood infection check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fast breathing | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Blue lips/nails | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Yellow/Green phlegm | Immediate Referral | Warm fluids |
| Mild dry cough | Refer if fever starts | Ginger water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Postpartum Pneumonia or Sepsis in the lungs is a major cause of maternal death. It requires hospital-grade antibiotics and oxygen.

query: ASHA reporting: A mother is 3 days postpartum and her urine is very dark, her skin looks yellow, and she has a high fever. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Yellowing of eyes and skin (Jaundice)**
- **Dark 'cola-colored' urine**
- High fever with shivering
- Vomiting and unable to keep food down

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Immediate referral - Suspected Hepatic Sepsis or severe infection. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Hydration is priority**: Give ORS or boiled water in small, frequent sips.
- Do NOT give any local 'yellow' herbs or powders for jaundice.
- Keep her **resting** on her left side.
- Check for any foul smell from the birth canal.
- **Rush to the District Hospital**; she needs liver function tests and IV treatment.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Yellow skin, dark urine, and fever after delivery"] --> B["Give small frequent sips of ORS for hydration"]
  B --> C["Prohibit any local 'jaundice' herbs or treatments"]
  C --> D["Check for abdominal pain or bad-smelling discharge"]
  D --> E["Position her on her left side for comfort"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Yellow skin/eyes | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Dark urine | Emergency Referral | Give ORS |
| Persistent vomiting | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Jaundice combined with fever after delivery is a very high-risk situation. It could be severe sepsis affecting the liver. Medical help is urgent.

query: 1 week after birth. The mother has a small, red, painful pimple on her belly that is getting bigger every day. She has a mild fever. Help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Spreading redness (larger than a coin)**
- **Skin feeling hot and hard** like a board
- Pus coming out
- Fever or body ache

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Apply warm compress and refer for skin infection check. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT squeeze** the pimple or lump; this pushes bacteria into the deep tissue.
- **Warm Compress**: Apply a clean, warm cloth to the area for 15 minutes, 3 times a day.
- Draw a **circle with a pen** around the red area to see if it grows bigger.
- Keep the area **clean** with mild soap and water.
- **Refer to PHC**; she may need antibiotics to prevent the infection from reaching her internal organs.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Spreading red painful lump on skin after delivery"] --> B["Do NOT squeeze or poke the area"]
  B --> C["Apply a clean warm compress to the area"]
  C --> D["Mark the boundary of redness with a pen"]
  D --> E["Check for fever or spreading of the mark"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for antibiotics and wound care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Redness spreading | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Fever | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Pus drainage | Refer to PHC | Warm compress |
| Small stable pimple | Monitor at home | Hygiene and warmth |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Skin infections (Cellulitis) in a new mother can spread rapidly because her immune system is weak. Early antibiotics prevent complications.

query: A mother who delivered at home 4 days ago has a very high fever. The family is not letting her take medicine because they say it will 'dry her milk'. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever > 102°F**
- Foul smell from discharge
- Mother is too weak to nurse
- Baby is crying constantly (mother's milk might be low due to fever)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Educate family and refer for life-saving antibiotics. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Explain to the family**: Antibiotics *save* the mother's life so she can continue to feed her baby. Fever and infection 'dry the milk' more than medicine does.
- **Ensure Hydration**: Give her **Jeera (Cumin) water** and **milk** to help with milk production while she is sick.
- **Sponge the fever**: Lower her temperature with cool water wipes.
- **Refer to PHC/CHC** immediately. Infection will not go away without proper medicine.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Family refusing medicine for mother's fever"] --> B["Explain that infection is what reduces milk, not medicine"]
  B --> C["Perform tepid sponging to lower the fever"]
  C --> D["Give Jeera water and fluids to support milk production"]
  D --> E["Check for foul smell or abdominal pain"]
  E --> F["Insist on referral to CHC for life-saving antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever + Foul smell | Emergency Referral | Never |
| High fever | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Low milk supply | Refer with mother | Increase fluids/nursing |
| Mild fever | Refer to PHC | Hydration and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Delaying antibiotics for a postpartum fever due to myths about breastfeeding is a major cause of maternal death. Most antibiotics are safe for nursing.

query: Is it okay for a mother with a fever to continue breastfeeding her baby?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Baby becomes weak or develops a fever**
- Mother is too confused or weak to hold the baby safely
- Pus coming out of the nipple

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Mother & Baby | Both | Continue breastfeeding unless told otherwise by a doctor. Refer mother for fever. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Continue Nursing**: The baby gets antibodies from the mother's milk to help *not* get the same infection.
- **Wash hands**: The mother must wash her hands with soap before touching the baby.
- **Wear a cloth mask**: If she has a cough, she should cover her mouth/nose with a clean cloth while nursing.
- **Refer the mother to PHC** to find the cause of the fever.
- If the mother is too weak, someone should help her hold the baby while she feeds.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Mother with fever wants to know if she can nurse"] --> B["Advise continued breastfeeding for baby's immunity"]
  B --> C["Instruct mother to wash hands with soap before nursing"]
  C --> D["Recommend wearing a clean cloth mask if she has a cough"]
  D --> E["Ensure mother gets plenty of fluids and rest"]
  E --> F["Refer mother to PHC to treat the underlying infection"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Mother very weak | Immediate Referral | Help her hold the baby |
| Pus from nipple | Immediate Referral | Stop nursing from that side |
| Fever starts | Refer to PHC | Continue nursing/fluids |
| Baby looks unwell | Immediate Referral | Urgent baby checkup |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In almost all cases, breastfeeding should continue during a maternal infection. It is the best protection for the baby.

query: A mother has a fever and her vaginal discharge has completely stopped 2 days after birth. Is this a problem?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sudden stop of discharge (Lochia) with fever**
- Severe, cramping belly pain
- Hard, swollen lower abdomen
- Shivering

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Immediate referral. The discharge is 'trapped' and causing infection. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Encourage movement**: If she is able, have her walk a few steps to help the womb drain.
- **Massage the belly**: Gently rub the lower belly to encourage the womb to contract and release the trapped blood.
- **Apply a warm compress** to the lower belly for pain relief.
- Do NOT give any tablets to stop bleeding.
- **Refer to CHC**; she may need a medical check to open the cervix and drain the infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Discharge stopped suddenly with fever and pain"] --> B["Have the mother walk a few steps if possible"]
  B --> C["Gently massage the lower belly in a circular motion"]
  C --> D["Apply a clean warm compress for pain relief"]
  D --> E["Provide fluids and check for shivering"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for womb drainage and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No discharge + Fever | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Severe cramping | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Shivering | Immediate Referral | Warm fluids |
| Mild pain, normal flow | Home Care | Rest and hygiene |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
If the postpartum discharge (Lochia) is trapped inside (Lochiometra), it will cause a severe infection very quickly. Medical drainage is needed.

query: ASHA worker reporting: A mother is 15 days postpartum. She has a high fever and a very bad headache. Her eyes look very pale. No discharge issues. What to check?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **High fever with severe headache**
- **Extremely pale eyelids and palms (Severe Anemia)**
- Fast heart rate
- Fainting when sitting up

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Immediate referral for both Anemia and Infection check. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check for Anemia**: Look at the inside of the eyelids. If white, she has severe anemia.
- **Provide Iron-rich foods**: Give Jaggery (Gur), Chana, and Moringa leaves if she can eat.
- **Hydration**: Give her ORS and fresh fruit juice.
- **Tepid Sponging**: Use for the fever to prevent a seizure.
- **Refer to CHC/Hospital**; an anemic mother cannot fight even a small infection and needs blood/IV help.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Fever, headache, and severe paleness after birth"] --> B["Check eyelids and palms for extreme paleness"]
  B --> C["Perform tepid sponging to manage the fever"]
  C --> D["Give jaggery and ORS for immediate support"]
  D --> E["Keep her lying flat to prevent fainting"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC for blood and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Extreme paleness + Fever | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fainting | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Pulse > 110 | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild paleness | Refer to PHC | Iron-rich local food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infection in an anemic mother is a deadly combination. Her heart is already under stress, and the infection can cause heart failure. Urgent hospital care is needed.

query: The mother delivered 3 days ago and now she is burning with fever. Her body is shaking (shivering) and she says her stomach is very sore. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever above 102°F with chills/shivering**
- **Foul-smelling vaginal discharge**
- Extreme pain in the lower belly
- Dizziness or fainting
- Fast heart rate

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Start tepid sponging, give fluids, and arrange immediate hospital transport. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cool the Body**: Wipe her forehead, neck, and armpits with a cloth soaked in room-temperature water.
- **Encourage Fluids**: Give ORS, coconut water, or plain boiled water to prevent dehydration.
- **Check Discharge**: Note if the lochia (bleeding) smells bad or has stopped suddenly.
- **Keep Clean**: Use only boiled and sun-dried cotton cloths as pads.
- **Refer Immediately**: Rush to the CHC; she needs IV antibiotics to stop the infection from spreading to her blood.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Mother has high fever and shivering post-delivery"] --> B["Perform tepid sponging with room-temperature water"]
  B --> C["Give ORS or coconut water for hydration"]
  C --> D["Check for foul smell or abdominal tenderness"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply any herbal pastes internally"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately for IV antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Shivering/Chills | Emergency Referral | Keep warm during travel |
| Foul-smelling discharge | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever > 101°F | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging only |
| Mild body ache | Refer to PHC | Rest and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fever after delivery is often a sign of 'Puerperal Sepsis.' It can be fatal within hours if not treated with medical antibiotics.

query: A mother is 1 week postpartum. Her breast is red, hard, and she has a high fever. She says it feels like a needle is poking her inside. Help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bright red, hot, and hard area on the breast**
- Fever with shivering
- Red streaks spreading from the breast
- Pus leaking from the nipple

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Lactating Mother | Female | Continue breastfeeding/expressing milk and refer to CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT stop breastfeeding**: Emptying the breast is the best way to clear the infection. Feed from the painful side first.
- **Warm Compress**: Place a warm cloth on the red area for 10 minutes before nursing to help milk flow.
- **Cold Compress**: Apply a cold cloth after nursing to reduce pain and swelling.
- **Gentle Massage**: While the baby feeds, gently stroke the hard area toward the nipple.
- **Refer to PHC**: She needs antibiotics to prevent a breast abscess (pus collection).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Red, hard breast with high fever"] --> B["Encourage frequent breastfeeding or hand expression"]
  B --> C["Apply warm compress before feeding to help flow"]
  C --> D["Apply cold compress after feeding for pain relief"]
  D --> E["Check for lumps or pus from the nipple"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for antibiotics and monitoring"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hard, painful lump | Immediate Referral | Warm compress |
| High fever/Chills | Immediate Referral | Continue nursing |
| Red streaks on skin | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Breast fullness only | Home Care | Frequent feeding |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Breast infection (Mastitis) can lead to a surgical emergency if pus forms. Do not wait more than 24 hours if fever persists.

query: 10 days after birth, the mother has a headache, fever, and a very stiff neck. She cannot look at the light. What is the emergency?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stiff neck (cannot touch chin to chest)**
- **Light sensitivity (eyes hurt in light)**
- Confusion or talking nonsense
- Severe, worsening headache

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Critical Emergency. Keep in a dark room and rush to District Hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Darken the Room**: Turn off lights or cover windows; light can trigger seizures.
- **Recovery Position**: Lay her on her side in case she vomits, to prevent choking.
- **Cool Sponging**: Use room-temperature water to lower the fever during transport.
- **Do NOT** give any food or water if she is confused.
- **Call 108**: This could be Meningitis or a brain infection. It needs immediate hospital care.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stiff neck, fever, and light sensitivity"] --> B["Place mother in a dark, quiet room immediately"]
  B --> C["Lay her on her side to prevent choking"]
  C --> D["Perform cool sponging for the high fever"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give anything by mouth"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stiff neck | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Confusion | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Light sensitivity | Emergency Referral | Darken the room |
| Headache + Fever | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
These symptoms suggest the infection has reached the brain or spinal cord. This is a life-threatening emergency.

query: The mother has a fever and it burns when she passes urine. She delivered at home 5 days ago. What should we do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe burning or pain during urination**
- Pain in the lower back (near kidneys)
- Fever with shivering
- Blood in the urine

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Increase fluids and refer to PHC for a urine test. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Force Fluids**: Tell her to drink one glass of water every hour.
- **Local Hygiene**: Wash the private area with warm water from front to back after every toilet visit.
- **Barley (Jau) Water**: Give her barley water or coconut water to help flush the bladder.
- **Check Belly**: Feel if the lower stomach is tender or hard.
- **Refer to PHC**: She needs specific antibiotics for a Urinary Tract Infection (UTI).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Painful urination and fever post-delivery"] --> B["Increase water intake to one glass per hour"]
  B --> C["Provide barley water or coconut water"]
  C --> D["Teach washing from front to back after toilet"]
  D --> E["Check for lower back pain or fever"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for urine test and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever + Back pain | Immediate Referral | Increase water intake |
| Blood in urine | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Burning sensation | Refer to PHC today | Drink plenty of water |
| Frequent urination | Refer if fever starts | Hygiene and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Untreated bladder infections can travel to the kidneys and cause permanent damage or blood infection (sepsis).

query: The family is giving the mother a very hot herbal 'kadha' for fever after birth, but she is getting more confused and her hands are cold. Should I stop them?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Confusion or talking nonsense**
- **Cold, clammy hands and feet with high fever**
- Rapid, shallow breathing
- No urine passed in 6 hours

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Critical Emergency. Treat for Septic Shock and rush to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the Kadha**: If she is confused, she might choke. Many herbs can also worsen dehydration.
- **Lay her flat**: Raise her legs 12 inches to keep blood in her head.
- **Keep her warm**: Use blankets for her cold hands/feet, but sponge her head if it is burning with fever.
- **Do NOT** give anything by mouth.
- **Emergency Transfer**: This is 'Septic Shock.' The infection is in her blood. She needs IV fluids immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Confusion and cold skin with high fever"] --> B["Lay mother flat and elevate her legs 12 inches"]
  B --> C["Stop all oral drinks including herbal Kadha"]
  C --> D["Cover with blankets to maintain body heat"]
  D --> E["Monitor breathing and pulse rate"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital for Sepsis"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confusion/Delirium | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cold skin + Fever | Emergency Referral | Keep warm only |
| Rapid breathing | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever only | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cold extremities in a feverish patient indicate the heart is failing due to infection. This is a life-and-death situation.

query: ASHA reporting: A mother is 4 days postpartum and her bleeding has completely stopped, but she has a high fever and severe stomach cramps. Is this bad?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sudden stop of postpartum discharge (Lochia)**
- **Fever above 102°F**
- Severe, sharp belly pain
- Hard, swollen lower abdomen

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Immediate referral. The infection is trapped inside the womb. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Encourage Movement**: If she can stand, have her walk a few steps to help the womb drain.
- **Belly Massage**: Gently rub the lower belly in a circular motion to encourage the womb to contract.
- **Hydration**: Give her ORS or warm water in small sips.
- **Tepid Sponging**: Lower her temperature with wet cloth wipes.
- **Refer to CHC**: She likely has 'Lochiometra' where the cervix is blocked, trapping infected blood inside.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Discharge stopped with high fever and pain"] --> B["Gently massage the lower belly in circles"]
  B --> C["Perform tepid sponging for the fever"]
  C --> D["Encourage a few steps of walking if possible"]
  D --> E["Provide fluids and check for shivering"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for womb drainage and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No discharge + Fever | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Severe cramping | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Shivering | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Mild pain with flow | Home Care | Rest and hygiene |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Trapped infected blood in the uterus causes rapid onset of sepsis. The cervix must be medically examined immediately.

query: A mother had a C-section last week. Today she has a fever and the wound is red with yellow pus coming out. What is the first aid?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Yellow or green pus from C-section wound**
- **Gaping (opening) of the wound**
- Redness spreading more than 2 inches from the cut
- Foul smell from the incision site

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Post-Surgical Mother | Female | Cover with sterile gauze and return to the hospital immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT squeeze**: Do not try to push the pus out. This can push infection deeper into the belly.
- **Keep it Dry**: Do not apply water, oils, or turmeric to the wound.
- **Cover it**: Use a sterile gauze or a clean, freshly ironed cloth to cover the area.
- **Rest**: Tell her to avoid all physical work and stairs.
- **Refer back to the hospital** where the surgery was performed today.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["C-section wound with pus and fever"] --> B["Cover wound with sterile gauze or clean cloth"]
  B --> C["Do NOT squeeze or apply any local pastes"]
  C --> D["Perform tepid sponging for the fever"]
  D --> E["Check for any spreading redness or gaping"]
  E --> F["Return immediately to the hospital of surgery"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pus leakage | Immediate Referral | Keep dry and covered |
| Wound opening | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Redness/Heat | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Fever | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
C-section wound infections can lead to internal organ infection (Peritonitis). A surgeon must evaluate the wound depth.

query: The mother is 12 days after birth and has a fever. One of her legs is much bigger than the other, red, and hurts when she walks. Help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **One leg swollen and red, the other looks normal**
- **Sharp pain in the calf** when walking
- Heat in the swollen area
- Shortness of breath (Critical sign)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Absolute bed rest, NO massage, and emergency hospital transfer. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Massage**: This is the most dangerous thing you can do. A massage can break a blood clot and send it to the heart.
- **Absolute Bed Rest**: She must not walk or even stand up.
- **Leg Elevation**: Keep the painful leg slightly raised on a soft pillow.
- **Check Breathing**: If she feels breathless or has chest pain, she needs oxygen immediately.
- **Rush to a Hospital**: This could be a DVT (blood clot), not just an infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["One-sided leg swelling and fever"] --> B["Prohibit any massage of the leg immediately"]
  B --> C["Enforce absolute bed rest; do not let her walk"]
  C --> D["Elevate the leg slightly on a soft pillow"]
  D --> E["Check for chest pain or breathing difficulty"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to hospital for clot check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| One-sided swelling | Emergency Referral | Never massage |
| Calf pain | Emergency Referral | Absolute rest |
| Shortness of breath | Critical Emergency | Call 108 |
| Fever only | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A blood clot after delivery is a 'silent killer.' It is often mistaken for a simple fever or muscle pain.

query: A mother delivered 3 days ago and has a fever. She says her private parts are very swollen and she passed a piece of 'rotten meat'. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Passing pieces of tissue (Placenta fragments)**
- **Extremely foul smell** from the vagina
- High fever with chills
- Continuous heavy bleeding

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Save the tissue and refer to CHC for manual womb cleaning. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Save the Tissue**: Put the piece in a clean bag or container to show the doctor.
- **Belly Massage**: Gently rub the top of the womb (fundus) to help it stay hard and push out any remaining pieces.
- **Hyration**: Give her ORS or warm water frequently.
- **Cleanliness**: Wash the external area with boiled, cooled water. Do NOT wash inside.
- **Refer to CHC**: She needs a D&C (cleaning) to prevent deadly sepsis.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Tissue passed with foul smell and fever"] --> B["Save the passed tissue in a clean container"]
  B --> C["Gently massage the top of the womb (fundus)"]
  C --> D["Give ORS or coconut water for hydration"]
  D --> E["Perform tepid sponging for high fever"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for manual cleaning and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Retained tissue | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Foul-smelling discharge | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Heavy bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Remaining placenta pieces will rot inside the mother and cause her to bleed to death or die of infection. Hospital cleaning is mandatory.

query: The mother is shivering uncontrollably 48 hours after delivery. She feels very cold but her head is burning with fever. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Uncontrollable shivering (Rigors)**
- High fever follow by sudden sweating
- Extreme weakness
- Fast heart rate

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Keep warm during shivering, sponge for fever, and refer to CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Warmth during Shivering**: Cover her with blankets until the shivering stops.
- **Sponge for Fever**: Once shivering stops and she feels hot, use room-temperature water wipes to cool her down.
- **Monitor Baby**: Have someone else hold the baby to keep the baby warm and safe while the mother is shaking.
- **Give Hot Tea/Tea**: Provide warm fluids for energy.
- **Refer to CHC**: Shivering (rigors) usually means the bacteria have entered the bloodstream (Bacteremia).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Uncontrollable shivering (rigors) post-delivery"] --> B["Cover with blankets until the shivering stops"]
  B --> C["Provide warm ginger tea or jaggery water"]
  C --> D["Check temperature; perform tepid sponging if high"]
  D --> E["Ensure baby is safe with another adult"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for blood infection checkup"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rigors/Shivering | Immediate Referral | Keep warm temporarily |
| Fever > 102°F | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Extreme weakness | Immediate Referral | Not safe at home |
| Mild chills | Refer to PHC | Warmth and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Shivering is a warning sign of a severe systemic infection. Do not wait for the next episode; seek medical help now.

query: A mother delivered in the field/outside and now has a high fever, stiff jaw, and back spasms. Help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stiff jaw (cannot open mouth/Lockjaw)**
- **Back arching or muscle spasms**
- High fever
- History of birth in unhygienic place

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Postpartum Mother | Female | Critical Emergency. Keep in a quiet, dark place and rush to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Absolute Quiet**: Noise and light can trigger painful spasms. Keep the room dark and silent.
- **Do NOT** force the mouth open or put anything between the teeth.
- **Lay her on her side** to prevent choking if she has a spasm.
- **Rush to District Hospital**: This is likely Tetanus. She needs specific injections and a ventilator.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stiff jaw and back spasms after unhygienic birth"] --> B["Place mother in a dark, silent room immediately"]
  B --> C["Lay her on her side to prevent choking"]
```
